links.rationale: Both demand drawing inferences and citing text evidence to support understanding; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, so it legitimately also anchors RI.4.1.
Ohio's 'refer to details and examples when explaining' is the same act as using text evidence to support a response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.4.1.
Both demand inference plus evidence; Ohio's added 'quote accurately' is a precision increase on the same skill; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, shared with RI.5.1.
Quoting accurately to explain a text is using text evidence to support an appropriate response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.5.1.
Ohio 2a (determine a theme from details) and TX 'infer basic themes supported by text evidence' are the same literary-elements skill at the same grade.
Covers only Ohio 2b (summarize the text); genre-neutral Response strand SE for summarizing, so it also anchors RI.4.2.
Ohio 2a (determine a theme from details) matches TX 'infer multiple themes within a text using text evidence'; TX's plural themes is a modest scope increase on the identical skill.
Covers only Ohio 2b (summarize the text); genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.5.2.
Both analyze characters from textual detail (thoughts, words, actions); TX narrows to interactions and change, so it covers the character half of Ohio's character/setting/event triple.
TX is the only setting SE and reaches Ohio's 'describe a setting' only through the narrower demand of explaining the setting's influence on plot.
Ohio explicitly cues 'how characters interact' and TX asks students to analyze relationships and conflicts among characters; same comparative character analysis.

Shared object is figurative language (simile, metaphor), but Ohio asks students to determine its meaning while TX asks how it achieves a purpose; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand, and TX's determine-word-meaning SEs are outside this candidate set.
Covers Ohio's drama half exactly: structural elements of drama including acts, scenes, and stage directions.
Covers Ohio's poem half (structural elements such as stanzas, rhyme, sound devices); cross-grade -1 because TX moves poetry SEs off structure and onto figurative language at grades 4-5.

Shared skill is analyzing text structure, but Ohio asks how parts fit to form a whole while TX asks how structure serves author's purpose; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand used because TX has no literary part-to-whole SE.
Both address point of view as a literary device; Ohio adds explaining differences among character perspectives, which TX leaves implicit; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand.

Both address narrator point of view (first/third person); Ohio's 'influence how events are described' goes slightly past TX's 'identify and understand'; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand.
Both analyze how visual/graphic features contribute to a text's purpose and effect; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand, which is the only TX home for this analysis.
Shared skill is comparing and contrasting ideas across multiple sources; genre-neutral Response strand, since TX has no across-texts theme-comparison SE at grade 4.
Shared skill is comparing and contrasting ideas across a variety of sources; genre-neutral Response strand.

Captures Ohio's theme-across-texts element ('infer multiple themes within and across texts'), but not the same-genre constraint; cross-grade +1, and note TX renumbers literary elements to item 7 at grade 6.

Matches only Ohio's second sentence (activate prior knowledge, make text-to-self and text-to-text connections); genre-neutral Comprehension strand; the text-complexity-band clause has no TX counterpart in this candidate set.
Matches only Ohio's second sentence (prior knowledge, text-to-self and text-to-text connections); genre-neutral Comprehension strand; the text-complexity-band clause is unmatched.
Both demand inference plus supporting evidence; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, deliberately shared with RL.4.1 because TX draws no RL/RI distinction here.
Referring to details and examples when explaining a text is using text evidence to support a response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RL.4.1.
Both demand inference plus evidence; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, shared with RL.5.1.
Quoting accurately to explain a text is using text evidence to support an appropriate response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RL.5.1.
TX 9D(i) 'the central idea with supporting evidence' is the informational-genre analogue of Ohio 2a (main idea supported by key details); TX's verb 'recognize' sits below Ohio's 'explain how'.
Evaluating details to determine key ideas is the reasoning Ohio 2a demands; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.
Covers only Ohio 2b (provide a summary); genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RL.4.2.
TX 9D(i) 'the central idea with supporting evidence' is the informational-genre analogue of Ohio 2a; TX 'recognize' sits below Ohio 'explain how'.
Evaluating details to determine key ideas matches Ohio 2a's main-ideas-from-key-details reasoning; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.
Covers only Ohio 2b (provide a summary); genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RL.5.2.
TX 9D(iii) organizational patterns in informational text is the informational-genre analogue of Ohio's 'describe the overall structure' of a text.
Shared skill is identifying and explaining a text's structure; TX ties it to author's purpose, which Ohio does not; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand.
TX 9D(iii) covers recognizing organizational patterns in one informational text, but Ohio's operative demand is comparing structure across two or more texts, which no TX SE asks for.

Shared skill is comparing and contrasting across sources; TX has no firsthand/secondhand account distinction anywhere, so the perspective-difference core of Ohio is uncovered; genre-neutral Response strand.
Shared skill is comparing and contrasting ideas across a variety of sources; TX does not name analysis of differing perspectives across accounts; genre-neutral Response strand.
Both analyze how print and graphic features (charts, diagrams, timelines) contribute to understanding and purpose; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand.
TX 9D(ii) names informational text features such as diagrams that support understanding, but only asks students to recognize them, not to interpret information they present.
TX 9E(ii) 'explaining how the author has used facts for an argument' is the direct argumentative-genre analogue of Ohio's 'explain how an author uses evidence to support particular points'.

TX 9E(ii) 'explaining how the author has used facts for or against an argument' matches Ohio's explain-how-evidence-supports-points, including the point/counterpoint discrimination Ohio adds at grade 5.
Ohio's 'integrate information from two texts to write or speak knowledgeably' is TX's 'synthesize information to create new understanding'; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.
Covers the write-about-multiple-sources half of Ohio, but TX frames it as comparing/contrasting rather than integrating; genre-neutral Response strand.
Ohio's 'integrate information from several texts to write or speak knowledgeably' is TX's 'synthesize information to create new understanding'; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.
Covers the write-about-multiple-sources half of Ohio, but TX frames it as comparing/contrasting rather than integrating; genre-neutral Response strand.

unmatched.why: Determining word meaning, including mythological allusions. TX housed vocabulary in its strand 3 SEs, which are not in the candidate set, and no reading SE here asks students to derive meaning from allusion. TX 4.9A names myths only as a genre to recognize, not as a source of word meaning.

Comparing a written story/drama to a visual or oral presentation of the same text. TX's only multimodal SE (4.9F) asks students to recognize characteristics of multimodal texts; it never asks them to compare a text against a performance or adaptation of it.

Explaining events, procedures, and concepts in a historical/scientific/technical text, 'what happened and why.' TX informational SEs (3.9D/4.9D) only ask students to recognize organizational patterns such as cause and effect as a structural feature; no TX SE asks students to explain the substantive content or causality of an informational text.

Explaining relationships/interactions between individuals, events, ideas, or concepts in a historical/scientific/technical text. Same gap as RI.4.3: TX treats compare/contrast and similar relations only as organizational patterns to recognize, never as content relationships to explain.

Determining meaning of general academic and domain-specific vocabulary. TX places this in its vocabulary strand (3), which is not in the candidate set. TX 4.7F only asks students to respond using newly acquired vocabulary, which is production, not meaning determination.

Determining meaning of general academic and domain-specific vocabulary. Same gap as RI.4.4: TX vocabulary SEs are outside the candidate set, and 5.7F is about using vocabulary rather than determining meaning.

Locating an answer quickly or solving a problem efficiently by drawing on multiple print/digital sources. This is a research/inquiry skill; TX houses it in its inquiry and research strand, which is not in the candidate set. TX 5.6H (synthesize) targets new understanding, not efficient retrieval.

Reading and comprehending informational text within a grade 4-5 complexity band. TX expresses reading volume/complexity through its self-sustained reading SE, which is not in the candidate set. Unlike RL.4.10, this standard has no prior-knowledge/connections clause, so there is nothing left for a genre-neutral Comprehension SE to match.

Reading and comprehending informational text at the high end of the grade 4-5 complexity band. Same gap as RI.4.10: no TX text-complexity SE in the candidate set, and no connections clause to anchor a genre-neutral match.
